title: Properties of the skyrmion crystal SkX-2 in the Heisenberg triangular lattice with scalar chirality
authors: H. Bocquet, C. J. Ganahl, M. Scheurer, P. M. Derlet, A. M. Läuchli
abstract: Skyrmion crystals have been primarily discovered under a magnetic field for materials with non-centrosymmetric interactions. More recent developments have investigated the stability of skyrmion crystals in itinerant magnets without magnetic field. In this study, we find that a type of skyrmion crystal with two topological charges per unit cell and no magnetization at the ferromagnetic point in reciprocal space, SkX-2, is naturally stabilized in an $SO(3)$-symmetric model with short-range interactions realized by the Heisenberg model on the triangular lattice with scalar chirality. We complement our numerical results with a theoretical analysis that quantitatively describes the transition from the ferromagnetic ground state to the SkX-2 and the evolution of the topological charge density. Despite the constraints given by the Mermin-Wagner theorem at finite temperature, the SkX-2 exhibits both a first-order phase transition associated with translation symmetry breaking and a continuous transition to a floating solid, depending on the charge density controlled by the model parameters. Finally, the tetrahedral phase supported by an antiferromagnetic interaction in our model is found to host $\mathbb{Z}_2$-vortices at finite temperature, suggesting the existence of an additional vortex topological transition.
summary: This paper explores a new type of skyrmion crystal (SkX-2) that is stabilized by scalar chirality rather than an external magnetic field. It details how this phase transitions between different types of order and how its melting is driven by the unbinding of topological defects. The work provides a complete picture of the phase diagram and thermal stability of these textures on a triangular lattice.
main_problem: The study investigates the stability, phase transitions, and thermal properties of a specific type of skyrmion crystal, SkX-2, in a magnetic system without an external magnetic field.
main_result: The researchers identify a SkX-2 phase with two topological charges per unit cell and demonstrate that it undergoes a first-order transition in the lattice regime and a continuous KTHNY-type transition to a floating solid in the free regime.
method: The study combines numerical optimization (basin hopping), Monte Carlo simulations (with replica exchange), and analytical approaches including Ginzburg-Landau theory and continuum expansion.
model_or_system: A classical SO(3)-symmetric Heisenberg model on a triangular lattice, augmented with a scalar chirality interaction term.
key_observables: Topological charge density, magnetization, heat capacity, Binder cumulant, spin-spin correlation functions, and defect density (5-fold, 7-fold, etc.).
important_parameters: Exchange interaction (J), scalar chirality interaction (J_chi), and the parameter theta = arctan(J_chi/J).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a classical spin model and uses a continuum expansion for the energy density and vortex energy derivations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows ground-state energies vs. theta; Figure 2 illustrates various spin configurations (AFM, tetrahedral, SkX-2, FM); Figure 4 shows the Binder parameter dip; Figure 5 shows heat capacity peaks; Figure 7 shows vortex energy scaling.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the identification of the SkX-2 ground state and its energy landscape, moves to the evolution of topological charge density via continuum expansion, then explores thermal phase transitions and defect-driven melting using Monte Carlo simulations, and concludes with analytical derivations of vortex energy.
why_interesting: The paper explores topological phase transitions (KTHNY and Z2-vortex unbinding) and the emergence of complex topological textures in a highly symmetric model, which is relevant to the study of many-body dynamics and topological order.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ground-state energies per spin of the Hamiltonian in Eq. 1 as a function of θ = arctan(Jχ/J). The solid lines rep- resent analytical ground-state energy curves, and crosses are the numerical values obtained using a basin hopping proce- dure for a doubly periodic triangular spin lattices of various sizes (between 6 × 6 and 36 × 36 spins) to limit boundary effects (only minimal energy is provided). The background colors mark different phases, where the SkX-2 region contains both, the lattice SkX-2 and the free SkX-2.

FIG. 2. Schematic view of the spins for a) the 120° order, b) the tetrahedral order, c) the lattice SkX-2 and d) the free SkX-2 (q ≈0.002/a2). The magnetic unit cells are highlighted by the pink quadrilateres. The inset in panel c) describes the nearest neighbour bonds Si · Sj in the lattice SkX-2 – grey (red) is (anti-)ferromagnetic. This demonstrates the lattice symmetry breaking of the lattice SkX-2. The inset in panel d) shows the distribution of charge density according to Eq. 7 — red (white) corresponds to a positive (absence of) charge density. The charge is not localized about different centers like in SkX-1. Nevertheless, every white center lacking charge comes along with half a...

FIG. 3. Topological charge density q as a function of θ for the ground state, whose energy is given in Fig. 2. The orange line corresponds to the charge density that minimizes the energy in the analytical model, as given by Eq. 12.
FIG. 4. Binder parameter for the lattice SkX-2 order param- eter on the 240×240-spins triangular lattice for θ = 51π/100.

FIG. 5. a) Heat capacity as a function of temperature for dif- ferent system sizes at θ = 1.96. The sharp peak that does not diverge with systems size at T = 0.17 indicates the presence of a distinct continuous feature. b) Spin-spin correlation func- tion at θ = 1.96 for different temperatures. The data up to a separation distance |r| = 70a indicates a change of behaviour from exponential to power law at T ≈0.17, suggesting the appearance of quasi-long range order persisting at least down to T = 0.10.
